project.title: La Cimbali Microcimbali
project.overview: The Microcimbali, nicknamed the baby Cimbali, is a small single group lever machine from the late 1950s designed for tiny bars and homes. This one came from a family who had inherited it and wanted it working again for the kitchen of their home rather than a commercial setting. Small machine, but every bit as much work as a big one.
project.objective: The family did not want a perfect showpiece. They wanted the machine their grandfather had used brought back to honest working order, with its character intact. A few honourable scars were to be kept. The priority was safety and reliability, since it would be used daily at home, and a finish that looked cared for rather than factory fresh.
project.process: Being a home machine that had been run hard, the wiring was the first concern, and I replaced it entirely to modern safety standards while keeping the original switch and look. The small boiler was descaled and resealed, and I rebuilt the compact lever group with new seals. The body had surface rust under flaking paint, so I took it back to bare metal, treated it, and repainted it in the original cream, deliberately stopping short of a mirror finish so it still read as a machine with a history. I kept the original badge, just cleaned, rather than replacing it.
project.impact: The Microcimbali is back on the family's kitchen counter, pulling the morning espresso just as it did decades ago. Keeping a few marks was the right call. It looks like what it is, a loved machine that has been carefully brought back rather than erased. These small domestic restorations are some of the most satisfying, because the machine goes straight back into daily life.
